summary: The post argues that Mercurial’s distributed model is fundamentally different from Subversion’s centralized, server‑dependent workflow. Ronacher explains that in Mercurial there is no special server, every clone is a full repository, and branching/merging are first‑class operations rather than painful exceptions. He walks through how cloning, pulling, updating, and merging work locally, and how configuration is stored in per‑repo `hgrc`. He emphasizes that contributions can flow through bundles, patchbombs, or ad‑hoc servers even without push access. The conclusion is pragmatic: Mercurial enables fast, resilient collaboration and makes it easy to keep history intact across contributors.
key_points: Subversion is centralized like FTP; Mercurial is distributed like BitTorrent with no special server.
Branching and merging are core workflows in Mercurial, avoiding huge, delayed changesets.
Cloning creates a full local repo with history; pulls create another branch that must be updated/merged.
Contributors without push rights can share bundles or serve their own repos for others to pull.
Distributed workflow makes teams resilient: work continues even if the central machine disappears.
money_quotes: Subversion works like FTP whereas mercurial is bittorrent.
All the revision information is available to anyone and there is absolutely no difference between server and clients.
This fundamental design decision means that there are dozens of separate branches of the code.
After you got your very own repository by cloning the pygments one you will notice that all the subversion-like commands ... work locally only.
A bundle stores multiple changesets in one file and it also preserves the correct author information and timestamps.
When I left everybody had all the changes locally because they pulled and I could remove my notebook and everybody continued working on their way home.
key_insight: Mercurial’s distributed design removes the server bottleneck, making branching, collaboration, and offline continuity natural rather than exceptional.
